1.synthesis: Paul names the Corinthians' immaturity bluntly: he could not address them "as spiritual people, but as people of the flesh, as infants in Christ," feeding them "milk, not solid food" (vv.1–2). The proof is their jealousy and strife: "While there is jealousy and strife among you, are you not of the flesh?" — saying "I follow Paul" and "I follow Apollos" (vv.3–4). But what are these leaders? Merely "servants through whom you believed." "I planted, Apollos watered, but God gave the growth" (vv.5–6). The one who plants and the one who waters are nothing; "only God who gives the growth" matters — for "you are God's field, God's building" (vv.7–9).
1.historical_context: The milk-and-solid-food image (v.2) was a common educational convention — Philo, Epictetus, and Plutarch all used it for the stages from beginner to advanced learner — and Paul applies it to expose the Corinthians' spiritual infancy despite their prized gifts. "Of the flesh" (sarkinoi, v.3) diagnoses their jealousy and strife as marks of flesh-controlled life, not Spirit-led maturity. By calling himself and Apollos "servants" (diakonoi, v.5), Paul deliberately strips away the patron status the Corinthians had assigned them, since in the patron-client system clients attached themselves to powerful figures for benefit. The farming metaphor (v.6) reflects the real history: Paul founded the church (Acts 18:1–11), Apollos later taught there (Acts 18:27–28).
1.literary_structure: Paul resumes the divisions theme of 1 Corinthians 1:10–12, now diagnosing the factions as evidence of immaturity (vv.1–4). The agricultural metaphor — plant, water, grow (vv.6–8) — recasts rival leaders as co-laborers in one field, dissolving the basis for partisanship. The repeated emphasis on God "who gives the growth" (v.7) is the theological heart, anti-patronal in thrust: the community owes its life to God, not to its founders' charisma. The closing metaphor-shift from "field" to "building" (v.9) sets up the construction imagery of vv.10–17.
1.theological_application: Factionalism is a symptom of spiritual immaturity, not advanced spirituality (v.3): the church that splinters into camps around favorite leaders has stalled in infancy, whatever its gifts. The cure is to see leaders rightly — as "servants through whom you believed" (v.5), instruments, not masters. Above all, growth is God's gift: "neither he who plants nor he who waters is anything, but only God who gives the growth" (v.7). This frees ministers from rivalry and pride and frees the church from idolizing them, locating all fruitfulness in God.
1.christology: The leaders are "God's fellow workers" and the church is "God's field, God's building" (v.9) — the people belong to God in Christ, not to any apostle. Faith came "through" servants but rests on the Lord in whom they believed (v.5). By insisting that only God gives growth, Paul points beyond every human worker to the risen Christ who builds his church, so that the community's existence is the fruit of his life-giving power, mediated but not produced by his servants.

10.synthesis: Shifting to architecture, Paul calls himself a "skilled master builder" who "laid a foundation," and warns each one to "take care how he builds upon it" (v.10). For "no one can lay a foundation other than that which is laid, which is Jesus Christ" (v.11). Whatever one builds — gold and precious stones, or wood and straw — "the Day will disclose it… and the fire will test what sort of work each one has done" (vv.12–13). Work that survives earns a reward; work burned up means loss, "though he himself will be saved, but only as through fire" (vv.14–15). And the community itself is sacred: "you are God's temple… If anyone destroys God's temple, God will destroy him" (vv.16–17).
10.historical_context: A "master builder" (architektōn, v.10) designed and supervised construction rather than laying every stone; Corinth, with its major building projects for the Isthmian Games, knew well that a building was only as sound as its foundation (v.11). Public buildings were prime displays of patronal status, their inscriptions naming the benefactor — but Paul removes the human name: it is "God's building" (v.9). Fire testing materials (v.13) draws on the prophetic image of divine judgment as fire (Malachi 3:2–3). "God's temple" (naos, v.16) is the inner sanctuary, the dwelling of God's presence — language the Qumran community also used of itself — now applied to the church indwelt by the Spirit.
10.literary_structure: The building metaphor introduced in v.9 is developed into a sustained image of foundation, materials, and testing fire. The argument moves in three steps: the one foundation (v.11), the testing of each builder's work at "the Day" (vv.13–15), and the warning against destroying the temple (vv.16–17). The fire that "tests" (v.13) ties back to the eschatological "Day of the Lord" theme of 1 Corinthians 1:8, and the temple imagery elevates the stakes of the factional quarreling: to fracture the church is to profane God's holy dwelling.
10.theological_application: There is only one foundation for the church and the Christian life — "Jesus Christ" (v.11) — and the warning "take care how you build" (v.10) presses every teacher and worker to build with lasting materials, for the Day will test the quality of all work (v.13). The sobering yet hopeful word of v.15 — saved "but only as through fire" — distinguishes the believer's secure salvation from the possible loss of reward for shoddy work. And the temple warning (v.17) reveals how seriously God regards those who damage his people through division or corruption.
10.christology: Christ is "the foundation" that is already laid (v.11): the church is not built on apostolic personalities or doctrinal novelties but on him alone, and every ministry is measured by its faithfulness to that footing. The testing "Day" (v.13) is the day of the Lord Jesus, who will assess each worker's labor. That the Spirit dwells in the church making it "God's temple" (v.16) is the fruit of Christ's saving work — the place of God's presence is now the people united to the crucified and risen Lord.

18.synthesis: Paul drives the wisdom argument to its conclusion: "Let no one deceive himself. If anyone among you thinks that he is wise in this age, let him become a fool that he may become wise" (v.18), for "the wisdom of this world is folly with God" (v.19). Therefore "let no one boast in men" (v.21) — and then the great reversal: "all things are yours, whether Paul or Apollos or Cephas or the world or life or death or the present or the future—all are yours, and you are Christ's, and Christ is God's" (vv.21–23). The Corinthians need not belong to a faction's leader; in Christ, every leader and all things already belong to them.
18.historical_context: "Become a fool that he may become wise" (v.18) returns to the cross-paradox of 1 Corinthians 1:18: true wisdom comes only through what the world counts foolish. Paul clinches it with two citations — "He catches the wise in their craftiness" (Job 5:13) and "The Lord knows the thoughts of the wise, that they are futile" (Psalms 94:11) — in vv.19–20. The climactic "all things are yours" (v.21) completely inverts the patron-client framework of Corinthian society: instead of being clients attached to apostle-patrons, the believers are the heirs to whom even the apostles, and the cosmos itself, belong.
18.literary_structure: This caps the entire argument of chapters 1–3, returning to the anti-boasting refrain ("let no one boast in men," v.21; cf. 1 Corinthians 1:31) and resolving the factions of 1 Corinthians 1:12 by naming the same leaders — "Paul or Apollos or Cephas" — now as the church's common possession (v.22). The sweeping catalogue (world, life, death, present, future) builds to the climactic chain of belonging: "you are Christ's, and Christ is God's" (v.23). This ascending order of ownership is the theological answer to all status-rivalry and rounds off the section's vision.
18.theological_application: The remedy for self-deceiving pride is the willingness to "become a fool" by the world's measure (v.18) — to abandon worldly wisdom as the path to God's wisdom. The astonishing "all things are yours" (v.21) liberates believers from petty partisanship: those who have Christ already possess everything, so there is nothing to gain by clinging to a faction. Belonging to Christ reorders one's whole relationship to leaders, possessions, life, and death — all of it is now a servant to the believer's good (cf. Romans 8:28), because the believer belongs to Christ.
18.christology: The closing chain — "you are Christ's, and Christ is God's" (v.23) — sets Christ as the one to whom believers belong and through whom they possess all things, while he himself is "God's," expressing the Son's loving submission to the Father within the unity of the Godhead. Because believers are united to Christ, the heir of all things, the whole cosmos serves their good (v.22). The factions that boasted in mere men are answered by the supremacy of Christ, in whom the church's true wealth and identity are found.
